verified_repairs.claim: Disabled legacy cron jobs in jobs.json read as explicit legacy/deactivated context rather than active authorities.
verified_repairs.details: All 6 disabled jobs have enabled=false and their payload messages explicitly say they are disabled legacy/historical audit context, not active runtime/authority.

verified_repairs.claim: loop_integrity_audit.py enforces a user-crontab ownership invariant that flags unexpected user crontab entries.
verified_repairs.details: The script defines unexpected_user_crontab_entries(), treats every meaningful user crontab line as unexpected because allowed_prefixes is empty, and appends a user-crontab-ownership error result when any such entry exists.

verified_repairs.claim: loop_integrity_latest.json reflects that invariant and currently reports no stray user-crontab entries.
verified_repairs.details: The latest integrity log contains a user-crontab-ownership result with status=ok and note that no stray user-crontab entries were present; direct crontab -l output was empty.

evidence.summary: Disabled jobs include Content Engine Agent, Community & Outreach Agent, seo-weekly, marketing-reflection, ralph-workflow-full-house-docs-audit, and docs-stack-aggressive-10min-self-heal; each is enabled=false and its prompt marks it as disabled legacy/historical audit context rather than current authority.
unexpected_user_crontab_entries() collects meaningful crontab lines, allowlists none, and the main flow emits a user-crontab-ownership error on any unexpected line or an ok note when none exist.
Latest log timestamp is 2026-05-21 10:56 UTC and includes user-crontab-ownership with note that Gateway owns the active scheduler topology and no stray user-crontab entries were present.
Direct command output at check time was empty (USER=mistlight, then no crontab lines).
